title: Latency spike & token/s drop impacting BrightCart — urgent
preview:
From: Sam Wong <sam.wong@brightcart.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-02-14T09:12:00Z
Subject: sudden p95/p99 increase + token/s collapse on hosted endpoint

Dylan —

We started seeing a sharp regression on our production calls to the hosted text-generation endpoint late last night (around 2026-02-13T18:00Z). p95 and p99 latency jumped ~3x and our observed tokens-per-second (TPS) dropped by ~60%. Error rate spiked to ~1.7% on some routes. This directly impacted customer checkouts (session timeouts).

We suspect this correlates with Redwood's performance optimizations you mentioned last week (quant/profile updates). Can you confirm whether any kernel selection / quant rollout happened around the 02/13 evening window? Attached are request samples and traces.

We need an ETA for mitigation; this is live-impacting. Please escalate.

—Sam W.
Head of Platform, BrightCart
Attachments: brightcart_p95_p99_traces_20260214.zip; request_samples_02-13-18_to_02-14-02.log

From: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Wong <sam.wong@brightcart.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-02-14T09:22:00Z
Subject: Re: sudden p95/p99 increase + token/s collapse on hosted endpoint

Sam — thanks for the immediate heads up and the artifacts. I'm escalating to eng and product.

Quick notes:
- We've opened incident BR-9831: https://console.redwood.ai/incidents/BR-9831
- Rishi, can you triage traces/metrics and confirm any rollout overlap?
- Laura, looping you in for potential credit/SLA decisions if this is rollout-related.

We will aim to have a mitigation plan within 2 hours and keep you updated every 30 minutes until resolved. Apologies for the disruption — we know this hits checkout directly.

—Dylan
Senior Account Lead, Redwood Inference

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-02-14T10:05:00Z
Subject: Re: sudden p95/p99 increase + token/s collapse on hosted endpoint

Dylan / Laura —

We dug into runtime telemetry and saw a change to kernel-selection heuristics and a quantization profile rollout that hit the hosted pool serving BrightCart's most-used r/w model at 2026-02-13T17:54:02Z (deploy ID: svc-optim-563).

Observations:
- The new kernel selection prefers a fused attention kernel on Ampere nodes for sequences >512 when a quant_profile flag is enabled. For several request shapes BrightCart uses (mixed short + medium contexts), the fused kernel increases per-request scheduling overhead and inhibits continuous batching, reducing effective token/s throughput by ~55–65% in our tests.
- p95/p99 regressions align with the kernel decision flip and the quant profile change window.
- Error bursts correspond to a fallback path where the fused kernel fails for some atypical layer shapes, triggering CPU fallback and amplified latency.

Immediate mitigation options (ranked):
1) Force-roll back quant_profile flag for the affected model variant (quickest, low risk).
2) Constrain kernel selection to previous heuristics for the hosted pool serving BrightCart (targeted, needs infra config push).
3) Canary a modified fused kernel with additional scheduling backpressure fixes (longer runway).

I'll prepare a rollback config and a small patch to force the previous kernel heuristic. If we roll back, we should see TPS recover to baseline within the next maintenance window (expect improvement in 30–60 minutes after rollout).

Attachments: internal_trace_diff_02-13_to_02-14.txt

—Rishi
Lead, Inference Runtime

From: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-02-14T11:20:00Z
Subject: Re: sudden p95/p99 increase + token/s collapse on hosted endpoint

Team —

This is heating up quickly and BrightCart is high-revenue. Please prioritize BR-9831:

1) Rishi — proceed with option #1 (rollback quant_profile for the BrightCart variant) now. Coordinate with ProdInfra and SRE for a fast config revert and monitor the p95/p99 and TPS metrics (baseline window: 02/13T16:30–17:30Z).
2) Dylan — notify BrightCart SLT that we're performing a targeted rollback and offer provisional credits for 02/13–02/14 outage window. Draft the short message and let me review.
3) Marissa / Sean — please hold a 30-min leadership bridge (summary + exec ask) once the rollback is in progress.

If rollback exposes other regressions, we will escalate to a hotfix and keep execs informed. We need a short postmortem within 72 hours explaining the kernel-selection logic gap and the safeguards we'll add.

—Laura
VP, Customer Success

From: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Wong <sam.wong@brightcart.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-02-16T17:03:00Z
Subject: Re: sudden p95/p99 increase + token/s collapse on hosted endpoint — resolution & next steps

Sam —

Thanks again for your patience. Summary of actions and outcome:

- Root cause: a quant_profile + kernel-selection heuristic rollout (deploy ID svc-optim-563) on 2026-02-13T17:54:02Z caused fused kernel selection for certain request shapes, which reduced continuous batching and lowered tokens-per-second while increasing p95/p99.
- Mitigation: We rolled back the quant_profile flag for BrightCart's hosted model variant on 2026-02-15T22:40:00Z and forced the prior kernel heuristic for the serving pool.
- Result: observed p95 improved to within 1.1x of baseline and p99 within 1.2x; TPS returned to ~95% of pre-regression throughput within 30 minutes of rollback. Error rate returned to baseline.

Attachments: postmortem-draft-BR-9831.pdf (link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/BR-9831-postmortem)

Next steps / asks:
1) We will provide BrightCart an account credit equivalent to the impacted window; Laura will finalize amounts.
2) Engineering will follow up with a short retention/telemetry change to detect kernel-selection-induced batching regressions earlier.
3) I'll share the 72-hour postmortem and schedule a 30-min sync to review corrective safeguards.

Please confirm if BrightCart is seeing stable behavior from your side. If anything odd appears, ping me immediately and we'll reopen the incident.

—Dylan
Senior Account Lead, Redwood Inference

title: Known-issues sprint: results + early metrics (W07) — publishing complete, early deflection, eng follow-ups
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>, Chris Osei <chris.osei@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 15, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Known-issues sprint: results + early metrics (W07) — publishing complete, early deflection, eng follow-ups

Team — quick leadership update on the CS/Support “known-issues sprint” (planning + execution + first adoption cleanup). We’ve finished publishing the initial batch and wired the cross-links into Support workflows.

1) What shipped (public/internal docs)
- Known Issues landing/index (Support/CS entry point)
  - Confluence landing: /customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/known-issues-index
  - Tagging + “when to escalate” callouts + last-updated discipline
- 5 workaround articles published (curated from top Q1 themes)
  - Streaming timeouts/disconnects + reconnect guidance (links from SDK troubleshooting)
  - 429 quota mismatch / rate-limit headers interpretation
  - Dedicated autoscaling oscillation (how to spot + config knobs)
  - Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations (rollback + what artifacts to collect)
  - Audit log delivery latency (expected SLO + interim mitigations)
- Escalation playbook refresh completed + linked from Support workflows
  - SLA policy by tier + severity mapping + Slack bridge conventions

2) Early metrics (directional, first week)
- Ticket deflection signals (Support):
  - 27 tickets in the last 5 business days where a macro included a known-issues link.
  - Of those, 11 were resolved without engineering escalation (customer confirmed workaround/mitigation in-thread).
  - 6 “repeat tickets” (same symptom, same customer) where the second ticket time-to-triage dropped materially (median first meaningful response down ~35–45% vs prior baseline on the same issue category).
- Time-to-mitigation (subset, escalations matching the published issues):
  - Streaming disconnects and audit log lag cases saw faster alignment on “next action” (workaround vs. incident bridge vs. SRE page). This is largely from having copy/paste-ready diagnostics + clearer escalation criteria.

Caveat: this is a small sample. Chris/Allison are setting up a cleaner ongoing dashboard so we’re not hand-counting.

3) Engineering follow-ups (asks)
To keep this from being a “docs-only” win, we need the associated product/infra follow-through. Top asks for the next 1–2 sprints:
- ENG-4825 (quota enforcement edge case + header consistency): please confirm rollout timeline + any risks of partial behavior by region.
- ENG-4824 (audit log lag metric/alert): align alert thresholds with enterprise expectations and document the runbook link that Support can use.
- ENG-4823 (support-safe dashboard links): canonical “quick view” dashboards for streaming health, latency, error rate, and audit log pipeline.
- ENG-4828 (private installer diagnostics bundle): ensure the diagnostics bundle collects exactly what Support requests in the KB article.
- ENG-4829 (autoscaling guardrails): confirm proposed defaults + whether there’s a safe backport path for existing Dedicated customers.

4) Next steps (ops)
- Dev + Elliot are rolling the macros refresh into the Support queue this week.
- Rachel + Jasmine to confirm we’re aligned on the SLA wording (esp. update cadence language) before we treat the doc as “policy.”

Attachments:
- Known-Issues-Sprint_W07_Results-and-Metrics.pdf
- known-issues_sprint_metrics_snapshot_2026-02-15.csv

If helpful, I can do a 15-min walk-through in staff this week focusing on the “what changed for on-call” angle.

— Jordan
Jordan Blake
Program Ops (CS/Support)
Redwood Inference

Date: Sun, Feb 15, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Known-issues sprint: results + early metrics (W07) — publishing complete, early deflection, eng follow-ups

Team — quick leadership update on the CS/Support “known-issues sprint” (planning + execution + first adoption cleanup). We’ve finished publishing the initial batch and wired the cross-links into Support workflows.

1) What shipped (public/internal docs)
- Known Issues landing/index (Support/CS entry point)
  - Confluence landing: /customer-success-and-support/known-issues-and-workarounds/known-issues-index
  - Tagging + “when to escalate” callouts + last-updated discipline
- 5 workaround articles published (curated from top Q1 themes)
  - Streaming timeouts/disconnects + reconnect guidance (links from SDK troubleshooting)
  - 429 quota mismatch / rate-limit headers interpretation
  - Dedicated autoscaling oscillation (how to spot + config knobs)
  - Private installer upgrade stuck at migrations (rollback + what artifacts to collect)
  - Audit log delivery latency (expected SLO + interim mitigations)
- Escalation playbook refresh completed + linked from Support workflows
  - SLA policy by tier + severity mapping + Slack bridge conventions

2) Early metrics (directional, first week)
- Ticket deflection signals (Support):
  - 27 tickets in the last 5 business days where a macro included a known-issues link.
  - Of those, 11 were resolved without engineering escalation (customer confirmed workaround/mitigation in-thread).
  - 6 “repeat tickets” (same symptom, same customer) where the second ticket time-to-triage dropped materially (median first meaningful response down ~35–45% vs prior baseline on the same issue category).
- Time-to-mitigation (subset, escalations matching the published issues):
  - Streaming disconnects and audit log lag cases saw faster alignment on “next action” (workaround vs. incident bridge vs. SRE page). This is largely from having copy/paste-ready diagnostics + clearer escalation criteria.

Caveat: this is a small sample. Chris/Allison are setting up a cleaner ongoing dashboard so we’re not hand-counting.

3) Engineering follow-ups (asks)
To keep this from being a “docs-only” win, we need the associated product/infra follow-through. Top asks for the next 1–2 sprints:
- ENG-4825 (quota enforcement edge case + header consistency): please confirm rollout timeline + any risks of partial behavior by region.
- ENG-4824 (audit log lag metric/alert): align alert thresholds with enterprise expectations and document the runbook link that Support can use.
- ENG-4823 (support-safe dashboard links): canonical “quick view” dashboards for streaming health, latency, error rate, and audit log pipeline.
- ENG-4828 (private installer diagnostics bundle): ensure the diagnostics bundle collects exactly what Support requests in the KB article.
- ENG-4829 (autoscaling guardrails): confirm proposed defaults + whether there’s a safe backport path for existing Dedicated customers.

4) Next steps (ops)
- Dev + Elliot are rolling the macros refresh into the Support queue this week.
- Rachel + Jasmine to confirm we’re aligned on the SLA wording (esp. update cadence language) before we treat the doc as “policy.”

Attachments:
- Known-Issues-Sprint_W07_Results-and-Metrics.pdf
- known-issues_sprint_metrics_snapshot_2026-02-15.csv

If helpful, I can do a 15-min walk-through in staff this week focusing on the “what changed for on-call” angle.

— Jordan

Redwood Inference

title: KiteWorks: Ephemeral buffer sizing and autoscale appendix for Dedicated deal
preview:
From: Liam O'Rourke <liam@kiteworks.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Cc: Asha Varma <asha@kiteworks.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Dedicated deal: burst runway and autoscale appendix

Hi Kimberly,

Thanks again for the demo last week — team was impressed. We're close on term sheet sign-off but have one commercial/ops constraint to land before procurement will push the PO: how transient burst capacity will be treated in the Dedicated contract.

Quick context: our traffic profile for July shows a recurring 20-minute window where inference spikes ~6-8x over baseline (attached forecast). We expect these peaks to be short and predictable (calendar-driven), but they exceed our committed slab. We need a pragmatic appendix that: 
- Defines ephemeral (burst) headroom behavior and crediting rules,
- Specifies autoscale thresholds and expected prewarm windows,
- Lays out eviction/priority rules if capacity is constrained, and
- Identifies any SLA credit triggers tied to failed burst attempts.

Can your team sketch an appendix we can attach to the MSA (we're targeting signature this month)? We're flexible on a small transient premium if it buys reliability.

I've attached the forecast (kiteworks-forecast.csv) and a short list of our desired peak percentiles. Happy to hop on a call if that helps.

Thanks,
Liam
Liam O'Rourke
Head of Platform
KiteWorks

Attachment: kiteworks-forecast.csv (csv)


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Cc: Liam O'Rourke <liam@kiteworks.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 11:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Dedicated deal: burst runway and autoscale appendix

All — looping you in to convert Liam's ask into a concrete appendix and ops runbook. Liam: forwarding to our infra/sales leads so we can be explicit about sizing and failure modes.

Planned next steps (proposed):
1) Karthik/Ben: run a short burst simulation using the attached forecast; return simulated tail latencies and expected token-cost uplift for 3 options: Minimal buffer (10% slab), Shoulder reserve (30% slab + prewarm 60s), Ephemeral buffer with credits (burst bank).
2) Draft: I'll produce a short appendix (1 page) that defines crediting rules and SLA triggers (proposal-draft-v1.pdf attached).
3) Sean: ops to validate runbook and confirm prewarm windows and any platform-level limits that would force eviction.
4) Legal/Procurement notes: if procurement needs a numeric cap or premium, let's propose a transient premium metric (e.g., $/vCPU-minute equivalent) rather than an unbounded burst.

Ben/Karthik — can you prioritize the sim today/tomorrow? I attached a params stub (redwood-burst-sim-params.json) with the forecast mapping.

Kimberly Park
Senior Director, Sales
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood-burst-sim-params.json (json), proposal-draft-v1.pdf (pdf)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Cc: Liam O'Rourke <liam@kiteworks.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 10:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Dedicated deal: burst runway and autoscale appendix

Thanks — ran three sims against the forecast. High-level findings (summary):

- Baseline: KiteWorks committed slab (X GPU equivalents) handles 95% of minute-level load outside events.
- Option A (10% minimal buffer): reduces immediate prewarm needs but at 99th percentile it still drops requests during sustained 15–25 minute spikes; latency tail increases ~2.3x.
- Option B (30% shoulder + 60s prewarm): consistently keeps tail latency within SLA for predictable windows but increases reserved cost ~18% over baseline. Prewarm warmup should be 45–75s depending on model KV cache warm-up.
- Option C (ephemeral buffer with tokenized credits): best for cost/latency tradeoff — allows short high-concurrency bursts with a credit balance that accrues monthly. Requires strict eviction priority and fallback model tiers to guarantee availability under contention.

Concrete autoscale policy we recommend for the appendix: 
- min_slab: commit size (no change),
- shoulder_reserve: 30% of committed slab (configurable),
- prewarm_trigger: when 1-minute moving avg > 70% of commit for two consecutive minutes, trigger prewarm (target 60s),
- ephemeral_burst_window: allow bursts up to 25 minutes aggregated per calendar event (configurable),
- crediting: allocate monthly burst credits (GPU-minute equivalent). Credits consumed first; if exhausted, fall back to shoulder_reserve; if shoulder exhausted, degrade to lower-tier compatible model (documented fallback);
- eviction_priority: ephemeral customers without credits are deprioritized ahead of pinned Dedicated customers; within kiteworks traffic, prioritize low-latency endpoints first.

Telemetry and SLA credit triggers: if an attempted prewarm fails to allocate capacity within 90s and client observes >20% request failures for >5 minutes, trigger a review and  service credit event (appendix to define credit formula).

I'll attach the sim params and a results CSV in the drive link. Ben — can you convert this into a short ops-friendly bullet appendix?

Karthik
Principal Infra Engineer, Redwood Inference

> Planned next steps (proposed):

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: Liam O'Rourke <liam@kiteworks.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 16:40:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Dedicated deal: burst runway and autoscale appendix

Sean — please review the draft runbook and validate operational assumptions below. I pasted key sections and attached the runbook draft (runbook-draft.md).

Operational assumptions we used:
- Prewarm time estimate: 60s (range 45–90s) to reach KV-cache steady state for the hopper model.
- Eviction: we will honor Dedicated pinned pools first, then shoulder_reserve, then ephemeral credits.
- Fallback: when ephemeral and shoulder exhausted, route to a quantized/cheaper model with degraded latency but the same functional behavior.
- Monitoring: a burst incident will surface in the capacity dashboard; automated paging threshold: sustained 5m error rate >10% during peak window.

Please add runbook steps for: emergency credit top-up (finance approval flow), manual prewarm (ops console command), and how we escalate to infra weekends.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Lead Reliability Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: runbook-draft.md (md)

To: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@kiteworks.com, Asha Varma <asha@kiteworks.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 17:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Dedicated deal: burst runway and autoscale appendix

Kimberly and team — this looks good and aligns with what our SREs discussed internally. Procurement attached a short list of questions (procurement-questions.pdf) but the high-level approach is approved by product/engineering: ephemeral credits + shoulder_reserve.

Two asks before we finalize appendix and routing to legal: 
1) Can we get a numeric cap on burst credit accumulation (we'd like a monthly cap to avoid runaway charges)?
2) Please include a sample SLA-credit calculation for failed prewarm (so procurement can quickly check cost exposure).

We are fine with a small transient premium and an explicit credit cap — will make the procurement flow much smoother. Once we have those two items, I'll route to our legal and procurement for final sign-off.

Thanks everyone — excited to get this live.

Liam
Head of Platform, KiteWorks

title: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed
preview:
From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 7:42 AM PST
Subject: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed
Attachments: Acme_streaming_escalation_summary_2026-02-07.pdf, Acme_customer_email_draft_v2.txt

Team — escalating Acme AI (enterprise) streaming timeouts/disconnects in us-east. They’re requesting (a) an exec-owned written update they can forward internally, and (b) clarity on potential credits given the customer impact.

I need approvals by 10:00am PT on:
1) Customer-facing language (attached draft) — specifically what we can state re: cause + “resolved” wording.
2) Whether we should proactively offer any goodwill / service credits vs. wait for their formal request + post-incident report.

Summary (details in PDF; ticket: SUP-1842):
- Customer: Acme AI (Corp)
- Symptom: SSE streaming responses resetting mid-generation (connection resets / truncated streams). Non-streaming less affected.
- Scope: us-east primarily; intermittent. Most visible on long generations.
- Current status: Mitigated overnight. Still monitoring; no new spike since ~00:10 PT.
- Mitigation: routing adjustments + gateway config rollback; recommended client-side timeout/retry guidance shared with Acme.

Customer asks / context:
- They have an internal exec review Monday and want a statement today.
- They asked for “credits for the incident window” on the call (Rachel has notes).

Proposed customer email language (high-level):
- Acknowledge impact + timeline
- Confirm mitigation applied
- Provide temporary client-side mitigation guidance
- Commit to follow-up RCA within 5 business days
- Avoid promising “fixed permanently” until we confirm longer soak

Please reply-all with:
- “Approved” / edits on the attached customer email
- Credits guidance (ok to offer X%, or wait)

Thanks,
Jasmine

—
Jasmine Liu
VP, Customer Success & Support
Redwood Inference



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 8:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed

Adding SRE technical color for customer-safe wording.

What happened (customer-safe):
- We saw an elevated rate of mid-stream disconnects for streaming requests in us-east, concentrated on longer-lived connections.
- Non-streaming requests had normal success rates; this was primarily a streaming transport stability issue.

Most likely cause (keep high level):
- An interaction between a gateway config change and connection handling under elevated concurrency in us-east led to increased connection resets for long-lived streaming responses.

What we did:
- Rolled back the relevant gateway config and applied a conservative keepalive/connection-handling adjustment.
- Confirmed streaming reset rate returned to baseline in us-east post-mitigation.

Confidence / cautions on wording:
- I’d avoid “root cause fully resolved” in the email. Better: “Mitigation applied; metrics back to baseline; monitoring closely.”
- We can commit to an RCA with preventative follow-ups.

If Acme asks for immediate guidance:
- Recommend setting an explicit client-side read timeout appropriate for their max generation length, and implementing retry with backoff on network disconnects.
- If they can, temporarily switch to HTTP/1.1 for streaming clients behind aggressive proxies (we’ve seen fewer resets).

Rafael



From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>

+1 to Rafael’s guidance.

On the comms draft:

  - “We identified a likely contributing factor in gateway connection handling under load and applied a rollback + configuration adjustment.”
  - “Streaming stability metrics have returned to baseline; we’re continuing to monitor.”
- Keep the “RCA within 5 business days” commitment.

On credits:
- Recommend we do not proactively promise credits in the same note as mitigation.
- Suggest language: “If you’d like to request service credits per your agreement, we can evaluate once we finalize the incident timeline and impact assessment.”

Reason: avoids creating a precedent / commitment before we have the full incident accounting and contract interpretation.

Summary (details in PDF; ticket: SUP-1842):
- Customer: Acme AI (Corp)
- Symptom: SSE streaming responses resetting mid-generation (connection resets / truncated streams). Non-streaming less affected.
- Scope: us-east primarily; intermittent. Most visible on long generations.
- Current status: Mitigated overnight. Still monitoring; no new spike since ~00:10 PT.
- Mitigation: routing adjustments + gateway config rollback; recommended client-side timeout/retry guidance shared with Acme.

Customer asks / context:
- They have an internal exec review Monday and want a statement today.
- They asked for “credits for the incident window” on the call (Rachel has notes).

Proposed customer email language (high-level):
- Acknowledge impact + timeline
- Confirm mitigation applied
- Provide temporary client-side mitigation guidance
- Commit to follow-up RCA within 5 business days
- Avoid promising “fixed permanently” until we confirm longer soak

Please reply-all with:
- “Approved” / edits on the attached customer email
- Credits guidance (ok to offer X%, or wait)

Thanks,
Jasmine

title: Re: SRE sign-off: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)
preview:
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

Hey Sean,

We’re locking the SLA policy + escalation triggers today so we can publish Confluence and wire up Jira automations (INT-333) without churn.

Can you take a pass on:
1) proposed Support SLAs by tier (first response + update cadence + escalation thresholds)
2) how the thresholds map to SRE paging + existing SLO alerting

Goal is to avoid any language that implies we page SRE outside of what on-call is actually set up to handle.

Attachments:
- Redwood Support SLA Policy - Draft v3.pdf
- Escalation Playbook - Severity Matrix v2.pdf

If you can reply with “approved” or edits by EOD, that’d unblock publishing.

Thanks,
Dev

—
Dev Patel
Support Ops Lead
Redwood Inference



From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 10:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

Thanks Dev — read through. We’re close, but I can’t sign off as-is because some of the phrasing will cause “page SRE for every P1” behavior that doesn’t match our paging policy or our current alerting.

Requested edits (blocking):

A) Separate “Support update cadence” from “SRE paging threshold” explicitly.
- Draft currently implies: enterprise updates every 30 min == page on-call immediately.
- That’s not how we operate. We page based on customer impact + service health signals, not the comms cadence.

Concrete text suggestion:
- Add a sentence under the SLA table: “Update cadence is owned by Support/CS and may occur while Engineering investigation is pending; paging is triggered by service-impact criteria below.”

B) P0/P1 definitions need to reference measurable impact signals.
Right now the examples are good, but the triggers are too subjective (“high revenue risk”, “multiple customers complaining”).

Please add 2-3 objective triggers that are easy for Support to cite in a page:
- Error-rate threshold sustained for 10+ minutes (e.g., 5xx or streaming disconnect classification above baseline)
- Region-level latency degradation relative to SLO (p95) sustained for 10+ minutes
- Single enterprise/dedicated/private customer completely blocked (auth outage, total unavailability)

C) After-hours policy must match coverage reality.
The draft says “Dedicated/Private: page SRE after-hours for P1 within 60 minutes.”
- That will create paging noise and “please investigate my integration issue at 2am” pages.

Suggested language:
- After-hours: only page SRE for P0 or for P1 that meets the objective service-impact triggers.
- Otherwise Support acknowledges + schedules next-business-day Eng handoff.

D) Don’t put a 30-minute update cadence as a blanket promise.
I know the intent is to be responsive, but we’ll fail it in any complex incident. If we want 30 min updates for a subset (e.g., exec escalations), it needs an “if materially new info OR at least hourly” carve-out.

Non-blocking but recommended:
- Add a “Waiting on Customer” note: SLA timers pause only for first response/update cadence if we’ve asked for specific artifacts and the customer hasn’t responded; we should be careful with the word “pause” in customer-facing language. Keep it internal.

If you want, I can do a redline in the doc, but the above is enough to update the Confluence version.

Sean

—
Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 10:27 AM PST
Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

+1 to Sean’s points.

Two extra details to keep us aligned with on-call:

1) Paging criteria should include “customer is blocked” explicitly for Private upgrades and auth.
- Example: Private installer stuck at migrations in production upgrade window = page the Private on-call / SRE if rollback guidance doesn’t resolve within 30–60 min.

2) We should avoid “page SRE to confirm dashboards” language.
Support can use the Support-safe dashboards (once ENG-4823 lands), but pages should include:
- region
- model or endpoint
- time window
- 2–3 request IDs
- what changed (SDK upgrade, config change, deploy)

If we capture that in the template, pages get answered faster.

Rafael

—
Rafael Mendes
SRE On-Call / Incident Response
Redwood Inference



From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 11:05 AM PST
Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

This is helpful. Two questions so I can align the enterprise language without overpromising:

1) For enterprise customers, can we keep “every 30 minutes” as an internal target for P0 only, and “hourly or when materially new info is available” for P1/P2?

2) For “Waiting on Customer,” we’ve had issues where Support keeps updating to avoid an SLA miss even when we’re blocked on logs. Are you OK with:
- internal SLA timer reminder continues, but update cadence becomes “next update after customer provides requested artifacts” (with a minimum check-in every X hours during business time)?

If that framing works, I’ll push edits into the policy language.

Rachel

—
Rachel Kim
Enterprise Customer Success Lead
Redwood Inference



From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 11:34 AM PST
Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

1) Yes — that’s aligned.
- P0: 30 min updates is fine as an internal target, but I’d still phrase it as “at least every 30 minutes while actively investigating/mitigating” (so if we’re in a stable monitoring state, hourly is acceptable).
- P1/P2: “hourly or when materially new info is available” is the right default.

2) Yes, but be careful with “next update after customer provides artifacts” as a blanket statement.
I’d suggest:
- Support continues to acknowledge at a steady cadence during business hours (e.g., every 4 hours) confirming we’re blocked and restating what’s needed.
- For enterprise P0/P1, do not go fully silent. Even if we’re blocked, we should provide “no new update; still awaiting X; here’s what we’ll do once received.”

And +1: keep the “pause SLA timers” concept internal only.

Sean



From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 4:38 PM PST
Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

Dev — assuming you incorporate the edits below, SRE signs off.

Sign-off conditions (please confirm in the published version):

1) Add explicit statement: Support SLA update cadence != paging threshold.
2) Add objective escalation triggers (error-rate/latency sustained; customer blocked) and put them above subjective triggers.
3) After-hours language: page SRE only for P0 or P1 that meets objective service-impact criteria.
4) Update cadence wording:
- P0: at least every 30 min while actively investigating/mitigating (hourly acceptable in stable monitoring)
- P1+: hourly or when materially new info is available
5) Template for pages includes required context (region, endpoint/model, time window, request IDs, what changed).

If you want a quick sanity check after you edit, paste the updated table and the after-hours paragraph in reply and I’ll do a final read.

Sean

—
Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference

- Single enterprise/dedicated/private customer completely blocked (auth outage, total unavailability)

C) After-hours policy must match coverage reality.
The draft says “Dedicated/Private: page SRE after-hours for P1 within 60 minutes.”
- That will create paging noise and “please investigate my integration issue at 2am” pages.

Suggested language:
- After-hours: only page SRE for P0 or for P1 that meets the objective service-impact triggers.
- Otherwise Support acknowledges + schedules next-business-day Eng handoff.

D) Don’t put a 30-minute update cadence as a blanket promise.
I know the intent is to be responsive, but we’ll fail it in any complex incident. If we want 30 min updates for a subset (e.g., exec escalations), it needs an “if materially new info OR at least hourly” carve-out.

Non-blocking but recommended:
- Add a “Waiting on Customer” note: SLA timers pause only for first response/update cadence if we’ve asked for specific artifacts and the customer hasn’t responded; we should be careful with the word “pause” in customer-facing language. Keep it internal.

If you want, I can do a redline in the doc, but the above is enough to update the Confluence version.

Sean

—
Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 6, 2026 at 10:27 AM PST
Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

+1 to Sean’s points.

Two extra details to keep us aligned with on-call:

1) Paging criteria should include “customer is blocked” explicitly for Private upgrades and auth.
- Example: Private installer stuck at migrations in production upgrade window = page the Private on-call / SRE if rollback guidance doesn’t resolve within 30–60 min.

2) We should avoid “page SRE to confirm dashboards” language.
Support can use the Support-safe dashboards (once ENG-4823 lands), but pages should include:
- region
- model or endpoint
- time window
- 2–3 request IDs
- what changed (SDK upgrade, config change, deploy)

If we capture that in the template, pages get answered faster.

Rafael

—
Rafael Mendes
SRE On-Call / Incident Response
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: SRE sign-off needed: Support SLA thresholds + escalation triggers (Playbook refresh)

This is helpful. Two questions so I can align the enterprise language without overpromising:

1) For enterprise customers, can we keep “every 30 minutes” as an internal target for P0 only, and “hourly or when materially new info is available” for P1/P2?

2) For “Waiting on Customer,” we’ve had issues where Support keeps updating to avoid an SLA miss even when we’re blocked on logs. Are you OK with:

2) Yes, but be careful with “next update after customer provides artifacts” as a blanket statement.

title: Quality gates: override policy escalation (post near-miss)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 3, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Quality gates: override policy escalation (post near-miss)

Team,

After yesterday’s near-miss (Optimize change nearly promoted without meeting gates), we need to tighten the override policy immediately.

Two goals:
1) Overrides remain possible for true emergencies (e.g., rollback unblockers), but
2) They cannot be a “convenience button” that bypasses quality and compliance.

Please reply today with:
- Who should be allowed to approve overrides (roles + named groups)
- Time bounds (how long an override can live)
- What must be recorded for audit (who/what/why + artifacts)
- Where enforcement lives (Optimize apply boundary vs rollout controller vs both)

We should also assume this will come up in enterprise security reviews. If our answer is “it’s logged somewhere,” that’s not sufficient.

Ava

—
Ava Chen
VP, Product & Platform
Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 3, 2026 at 9:46 AM PST
Subject: Re: Quality gates: override policy escalation (post near-miss)

Proposed policy (MVP → tighten further at GA if needed):

Approvers:
- Only “Optimize Override Approver” role (not general admin), initially: Optimize oncall (rotation) + Optimize PM lead (me) as backup.
- For enterprise accounts, require a second approver (SRE oncall or Security) depending on reason code.

Time bounds:
- Default 24h expiration.
- Max 72h (requires escalation beyond oncall).

Required evidence (must be attached/linked in the override request):
- Gate run id + summary (pass/fail deltas + flake score)
- The exact config diff hash
- Reason code (prod incident, capacity, false positive/flaky suite, customer block)
- Ticket reference (INC/SUP/INT)

Audit:
- Emit audit events on: override requested, override approved/denied, override expired, override revoked.
- Include approver identity + RBAC role + timestamps.

Enforcement:
- Block at Optimize “apply config” boundary AND rollout controller should refuse canary promotion without gate=passed OR override=active.

If we align on this today, we can update PM-482 and coordinate RBAC via INT-3391.

Aditya

—
Aditya Rao
Product, Optimize


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 3, 2026 at 10:18 AM PST
Subject: Re: Quality gates: override policy escalation (post near-miss)

+1 on defense-in-depth.

Security requirements I’d like to see explicitly:

1) Two-person rule when the override would allow production traffic to run on a config that failed quality gates.
   - One approver can be Product (Optimize), second should be SRE or Security (depending on reason). This avoids “PM approves their own ship.”

title: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 9:08 AM PST
Subject: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Team — sharing a quick exec update on the serving-runtime tail latency workstream (KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction) since it’s now gating both SLO confidence and a couple of late-stage conversations.

Why this matters (business + technical):
- SLO / reliability: We’re seeing burst-driven p95/p99 spikes in sustained decode workloads with mixed sequence lengths, especially when request churn leaves the KV arena fragmented. In the worst case it creates opportunistic evictions at bad times and can tip us into OOM / request shedding.
- Cost: Today we’re compensating by leaving more headroom than we want (lower effective utilization). If we can keep KV memory contiguous/predictable under load, we should need less “safety padding” in dedicated pools and can be more aggressive on hosted concurrency without tail regressions.
- Customer pressure: This pattern shows up repeatedly in support and in enterprise eval questions (see SUP-28411, SUP-28433). PM has asked for a credible plan and crisp risk story.

What we’re doing:
- Allocator + fragmentation measurement: improve page/block layout + free-list management; add a fragmentation ratio metric that correlates with p99 spikes.
- Budgeted compaction: incremental compaction with an explicit time/bytes budget tied to scheduler boundaries (prefill/decode transitions) to avoid decode stalls.
- Tier-aware eviction: last-resort pressure relief that is safe + observable; interacts cleanly with pinned sessions.
- Guardrails: feature flag, kill-switch, backpressure integration, and rollout policy that auto-rolls back on tail regression.

Timeline (targeting runtime-1.22 train):
- Jan 20–31: discovery/measurement + final design/ADRs (in review now)
- Feb 3–21: implementation (allocator changes + scheduler hook + metrics + initial eviction policy)
- Feb 24–Mar 7: validation (benchmark lab + perf canary + stress/conformance)
- Mar 10+: staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → dedicated pools)

Risk management (what I want exec eyes on):
- Primary risk is introducing copy overhead / stalls during compaction. Mitigation is strict budgeting and scheduler-phase enforcement.
- Secondary risk is correctness edge cases (cancelled requests mid-relocation, pinned sessions). Mitigation is stress tests + conservative stopping conditions + kill-switch.
- We will hold rollout if: p99 up > agreed threshold, eviction rate spikes, or compaction time/bytes moved exceeds budget in steady state.

Asks:
1) Mateo/Sean: please confirm you’re aligned on “stop the line” thresholds + who has authority to flip the kill-switch during a canary.
2) Connor/Talia: I’d like this treated as a first-class item on the runtime-1.22 release readiness checklist.
3) Starting next week: weekly status note to this thread (wins, risks, current metrics, next gates). Keep it short.

Attachments:
- KV_Compaction_Review_Slides_v3.pdf
- Runtime_1.22_Rollout_Guardrails_Summary.docx

Ava Chen
VP, Product & Platform
Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Aligned.

A couple notes to help frame risk:
- We are not doing “stop-the-world” defragmentation. Compaction is incremental with a hard budget (bytes moved + wall time) and can no-op if we’re in a latency-sensitive phase.
- Eviction is a pressure valve, not the primary mechanism. We expect it to be near-zero in steady state; if eviction is non-trivial, that’s a signal we should treat as capacity/traffic policy rather than “normal.”
- Omar + Sofia are owning correctness + scheduler integration; Noah is doing architecture/correctness review on the invariants.

Re: timeline, I think Feb implementation window is realistic provided we keep scope to allocator + minimal copy/pack kernels.

Mateo Alvarez
VP Runtime
Redwood Inference


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

+1.

On the guardrails / authority question:
- During canary, oncall SRE has authority to flip the kill-switch if we hit rollback criteria (p99 sustained breach + compaction/eviction correlated) without waiting for runtime to be awake. Runtime gets paged/escalated in parallel.
- We’ll codify in the rollout policy: rollback on (a) p99 regression over baseline for N minutes, OR (b) compaction time budget violations + elevated tail, OR (c) eviction rate > threshold for N minutes.

Allison + Rafael are helping make sure the dashboards/alerts are in place before we expand beyond internal canary.

Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 12:41 PM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Thanks — that authority model works.

For the weekly status: please start Monday EOD (Jan 26) with a simple template:
- Current state: frag ratio trend + p95/p99 deltas (if available)
- Last week shipped: PRs landed / metrics added
- This week: next gate + owners
- Risks / asks (1–2 bullets)

Mateo, please have Omar post it (you can forward if needed). Sean, if anything changes on rollback criteria/alert thresholds during canary, call it out in the same update.

Ava

Team — sharing a quick exec update on the serving-runtime tail latency workstream (KV cache compaction + tier-aware eviction) since it’s now gating both SLO confidence and a couple of late-stage conversations.

Why this matters (business + technical):
- SLO / reliability: We’re seeing burst-driven p95/p99 spikes in sustained decode workloads with mixed sequence lengths, especially when request churn leaves the KV arena fragmented. In the worst case it creates opportunistic evictions at bad times and can tip us into OOM / request shedding.
- Cost: Today we’re compensating by leaving more headroom than we want (lower effective utilization). If we can keep KV memory contiguous/predictable under load, we should need less “safety padding” in dedicated pools and can be more aggressive on hosted concurrency without tail regressions.
- Customer pressure: This pattern shows up repeatedly in support and in enterprise eval questions (see SUP-28411, SUP-28433). PM has asked for a credible plan and crisp risk story.

What we’re doing:
- Allocator + fragmentation measurement: improve page/block layout + free-list management; add a fragmentation ratio metric that correlates with p99 spikes.
- Budgeted compaction: incremental compaction with an explicit time/bytes budget tied to scheduler boundaries (prefill/decode transitions) to avoid decode stalls.
- Tier-aware eviction: last-resort pressure relief that is safe + observable; interacts cleanly with pinned sessions.
- Guardrails: feature flag, kill-switch, backpressure integration, and rollout policy that auto-rolls back on tail regression.

Timeline (targeting runtime-1.22 train):
- Jan 20–31: discovery/measurement + final design/ADRs (in review now)
- Feb 3–21: implementation (allocator changes + scheduler hook + metrics + initial eviction policy)
- Feb 24–Mar 7: validation (benchmark lab + perf canary + stress/conformance)
- Mar 10+: staged rollout (internal canary → hosted subset → dedicated pools)

Risk management (what I want exec eyes on):
- Primary risk is introducing copy overhead / stalls during compaction. Mitigation is strict budgeting and scheduler-phase enforcement.
- Secondary risk is correctness edge cases (cancelled requests mid-relocation, pinned sessions). Mitigation is stress tests + conservative stopping conditions + kill-switch.
- We will hold rollout if: p99 up > agreed threshold, eviction rate spikes, or compaction time/bytes moved exceeds budget in steady state.

Asks:
1) Mateo/Sean: please confirm you’re aligned on “stop the line” thresholds + who has authority to flip the kill-switch during a canary.
2) Connor/Talia: I’d like this treated as a first-class item on the runtime-1.22 release readiness checklist.
3) Starting next week: weekly status note to this thread (wins, risks, current metrics, next gates). Keep it short.

Attachments:
- KV_Compaction_Review_Slides_v3.pdf
- Runtime_1.22_Rollout_Guardrails_Summary.docx

Ava Chen
VP, Product & Platform
Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia.ivanova@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

Aligned.

A couple notes to help frame risk:
- We are not doing “stop-the-world” defragmentation. Compaction is incremental with a hard budget (bytes moved + wall time) and can no-op if we’re in a latency-sensitive phase.
- Eviction is a pressure valve, not the primary mechanism. We expect it to be near-zero in steady state; if eviction is non-trivial, that’s a signal we should treat as capacity/traffic policy rather than “normal.”
- Omar + Sofia are owning correctness + scheduler integration; Noah is doing architecture/correctness review on the invariants.

Re: timeline, I think Feb implementation window is realistic provided we keep scope to allocator + minimal copy/pack kernels.

Mateo Alvarez
VP Runtime
Redwood Inference

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>

Date: Thu, Jan 22, 2026 at 11:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec update: Serving runtime tail latency investment (KV cache compaction) — timeline + risk management

+1.

On the guardrails / authority question:
- During canary, oncall SRE has authority to flip the kill-switch if we hit rollback criteria (p99 sustained breach + compaction/eviction correlated) without waiting for runtime to be awake. Runtime gets paged/escalated in parallel.
- We’ll codify in the rollout policy: rollback on (a) p99 regression over baseline for N minutes, OR (b) compaction time budget violations + elevated tail, OR (c) eviction rate > threshold for N minutes.

Allison + Rafael are helping make sure the dashboards/alerts are in place before we expand beyond internal canary.

Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference

title: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)
Attachments: 2026-01-15-controlled-failover-metrics-snapshot.pdf, gameday-action-items-2026-01-16.csv

Team — sharing a concise leadership readout from Thursday’s controlled regional failover (us-east → eu-west) and what we’re doing next.

TL;DR
- The core automation path worked end-to-end (signal → controller decision → routing policy flip → traffic shift → stabilization).
- Customer impact was limited but not zero: brief elevated 5xx/timeouts for a small slice of Hosted traffic + streaming disconnects for some clients that don’t handle redirects cleanly.
- We found several “must-fix before wider rollout” items (anti-flap tuning, dashboard annotations, streaming reconnect guidance, and capacity headroom guardrails).

What we tested
- Scenario: planned impairment simulation in us-east and controlled failover to eu-west for a scoped set of routes/models and a limited tenant allowlist.
- Objective: validate (1) detection + decisioning speed, (2) safe automation/rollback behavior, (3) observability + on-call usability, and (4) Dedicated protection (no unexpected pool drain).

Results against success criteria (high level)
- Detection + failover time:
  - First corroborated impairment signal observed: ~T+75s from impairment start.
  - Policy flip executed: ~T+2m40s.
  - Traffic settled in eu-west: ~T+4–5m (client retry/backoff dominated tail).
- Error/latency impact:
  - Hosted API: short spike in 5xx/timeouts during the shift window; elevated tail latency in eu-west for ~12–18 minutes as retries + warm-up converged.
  - Dedicated: no unplanned customer pool drain; no sustained SLO-relevant impact observed.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - No uncontrolled oscillation, but we did see an automated rollback trigger earlier than intended due to hold-down/threshold interaction (details below).

Customer impact / support signals
- One customer reported transient 5xx/timeouts during the planned window (SUP-25310).
- Streaming disconnects were reported by a couple customers whose clients did not gracefully reconnect on redirect (SUP-25322).
- Follow-on ticket on elevated tail latency in eu-west immediately post-shift (SUP-25341) — correlates with headroom + retry storm.

What went well
- On-call coordination and go/no-go discipline were strong (checklist completion and rollback readiness were clear).
- Region health aggregation + policy engine evaluation produced explainable decisions (we could point to which signals tripped).
- Dedicated isolation worked as expected (Hosted shift did not inadvertently trigger Dedicated drain behaviors).

Issues found (key)
1) Observability gap: dashboards lacked consistent automated annotations for: impairment start, policy flip, rollback evaluation points. This made timeline reconstruction slower than it should be.
2) Anti-flap / rollback tuning: hold-down timer and corroboration windows were too aggressive in one segment, resulting in an early rollback evaluation while the region was still stabilizing.
3) Tail latency in eu-west after shift: headroom assumptions were optimistic for the specific routes/models in scope; combined with client retry behavior this caused a brief tail spike.
4) Streaming client behavior: redirect/reconnect handling still causes user-visible disconnects for some SDK versions / client patterns.

Decisions / next steps
- We are NOT expanding automation scope until the above items are closed and we pass a smaller-blast-radius rehearsal.
- We will schedule Game Day #2 with tighter allowlist + synthetic load shaping, and explicitly measure: failover time, retry amplification, and “time to steady-state p99”.

Action items (short list; full tracker linked)
- Add automation-driven dashboard annotations + incident timeline hooks (Owner: Sean / Observability; target: end of Jan)
- Retune rollback/hold-down thresholds and add “stabilization grace” logic (Owner: Rafael + Routing; target: end of Jan)
- Capacity guardrail: require minimum eu-west headroom for routes in allowlist before executing flip (Owner: Irene + Fleet; target: early Feb)
- Streaming: finalize redirect-safe reconnect behavior + publish SDK guidance (Owner: Priya + Runtime/Gateway; target: early Feb)
- Comms: align Support macros + status-page language for planned tests (Owner: Sean; target: before next test)

Links
- Results page (metrics + timeline): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Controlled+Failover+Test+Results+-+2026-01-15
- Draft postmortem (focus on regressions + prevention): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Postmortem%3A+Controlled+Failover+Test+Regression
- Action item tracker (ENG-2426): https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2426/post-gameday-action-items

Asks
1) Approval to hold Game Day #2 in early Feb (exact date TBD) once the above gating items are closed.
2) Confirmation that we should proactively notify the same set of enterprise accounts as last time (Support has the list), or expand/contract based on risk appetite.

Thanks to everyone who helped make the test controlled and safe — we learned the right things without a large blast radius.

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 10:03 AM PT
Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

This is the right framing.

Two adds:
- Let’s treat “dashboard annotations” as a hard gate for the next test. We lost time during the live window correlating graphs to events.
- Support comms: I’d prefer we keep proactive notifications limited to the same cohort (largest Hosted + Dedicated accounts + anyone with data residency questions) until streaming reconnect guidance is fully out.

I’ll sync with Rafael on gating criteria and get a proposed Feb window on the calendar once ENG-2426 is green.

— Sean
Sean Gallagher
Director, Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference


On Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 10:26 AM Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 10:26 AM PT
Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

Thanks, Ava.

A couple questions before I approve another production test window:
1) Were there any indications we violated (or came close to violating) the multi-region availability SLO budget during the test window?
2) What is our plan to ensure the next test doesn’t create a retry amplification event in the target region? (I want this explicitly called out in go/no-go.)

If we can answer those clearly and keep blast radius limited, I’m supportive of early Feb.

— Neha
Neha Kapoor
VP, Infrastructure & Reliability
Redwood Inference


On Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 12:05 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 12:05 PM PT
Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

Answers inline.

1) SLO/error budget proximity
- We did not materially burn the multi-region availability SLO budget for the month.
- The spike was brief and scoped; the rolling burn-rate alerts triggered as expected, but did not cross paging thresholds for sustained windows.
- We’ll include the exact numbers in the postmortem once the annotation gap is fixed (so we can line up the windows precisely), but this was within the planned “acceptable test impact” bounds.

2) Preventing retry amplification in the target region (explicit go/no-go)
We’re adding three concrete gates before we run Game Day #2:
- Capacity/headroom gate: require minimum eu-west headroom for the exact route/model allowlist (not just aggregate regional headroom). Irene’s team is helping define thresholds.
- Gateway/backoff gate: validate retry budgets and Retry-After behavior in staging under redirect (Priya’s team + Peter’s circuit breaker changes), with a target “max retry amplification” metric.
- Synthetic load rehearsal: replay a shaped load pattern with perf canaries enabled and confirm p99 stabilization time is within target before touching prod.

If those three are green, we’ll propose an early Feb date with an even smaller allowlist than 1/15.

I’ll also make sure the go/no-go doc includes an explicit rollback trigger tied to retry amplification (not just error burn).

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference

title: Sign-off request: DNS TTL + traffic steering updates for regional failover (Terraform PR-1442)
preview:
From: Victor Alvarez <victor.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@redwoodinference.com>, Miles Thornton <miles.thornton@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Sign-off request: DNS TTL + traffic steering updates for regional failover (Terraform PR-1442)

Hi Irene,

Requesting infra leadership sign-off for the DNS TTL + traffic steering changes we need before the controlled failover game day (US-East -> EU-West) tomorrow.

Change summary (PR-1442):
- Lower DNS TTL for api.redwoodinference.com and dedicated-gw.redwoodinference.com records from 60s -> 20s (Route 53 alias + health-checked records).
- Enable weighted routing policy targets for “failover-ready” regions (default 100/0, controller can flip to 0/100).
- Add explicit “hold-down” during flips (via routing controller; infra side is just supporting records + constraints).
- Add resolver-cache jitter note in runbook (we cannot control client resolvers, but lower TTL materially improves time-to-shift for the long tail).

Risk assessment (high level):
1) Risk: unintended traffic shift due to misconfigured record weights/health checks.
   Mitigation: staged apply in prod (plan-only review, then apply w/ Victor + Emily on bridge), hard-coded default weights pinned in Terraform, and a one-command rollback (revert commit + apply).

2) Risk: resolvers ignoring low TTL / caching longer, making failover appear “slow” and leading to repeated flips.
   Mitigation: controller anti-flap + rate limits (ADR-043); we will *not* treat “not all clients moved” as a failure condition within the first N minutes. Observability will track ‘traffic moved’ vs ‘resolvers still pinned’.

3) Risk: increased DNS query rate / cost.
   Mitigation: expected increase is bounded; we validated on staging + last 30d QPS. Also, TTL 20s is temporary until we have more confidence; can raise to 30–45s with no functional changes.

4) Risk: interacting with Anycast/BGP steering and provider edge behavior.
   Mitigation: we are not changing any BGP announcements. This is DNS-only + routing policy weights.

Proposed execution:
- Today: sign-off + Terraform apply in prod during low-traffic window (target 17:00–18:00 UTC).
- Verify: synthetic reachability probes + resolver sampling + gateway metrics.
- Tomorrow: game day will use controller dry-run first; only flip traffic with go/no-go from Sean.

Asks:

From: Victor Alvarez <victor.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@redwoodinference.com>, Miles Thornton <miles.thornton@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 12:26 PM PST
Subject: Re: Sign-off request: DNS TTL + traffic steering updates for regional failover (Terraform PR-1442)

All good questions — answers inline.

1) Blast radius / scoping
- Hosted API: api.redwoodinference.com is the default endpoint for all Hosted API customers.
- Dedicated: dedicated-gw.redwoodinference.com is the shared gateway endpoint used by Dedicated tenants unless they have a pinned custom domain. (Custom domains are out of scope for tomorrow’s test.)
- So yes, it’s broad. However, the change is TTL + record policy only; we are *not* shifting traffic by default. We are establishing the ability to shift faster when the controller flips weights.

Hard rollback trigger proposal:
- Within 15 minutes post-apply, if we see any of the below at global level (aggregated):
  - >0.5% increase in 5xx over baseline for 5 consecutive minutes (gateway)
  - p99 latency +20% over baseline for 10 consecutive minutes (gateway)
  - synthetic probe reachability <99.5% for 5 consecutive minutes (perf-canary)
  - Route 53 health check anomalies: >5% of checks reporting unhealthy for a region while perf-canary reachability is healthy (this indicates health-check false positive)

2) Health checks failure modes
- We are not using “health check red => automatic failover.” The controller’s trigger hierarchy treats reachability (perf-canary) as higher-confidence than R53 health checks.
- Route 53 health checks are used only as a corroboration signal and to prevent routing to a region we *know* is unreachable from multiple vantage points.
- If Route 53 health checks misbehave (false negatives), weights remain unchanged unless controller sees corroboration. In other words: R53 cannot unilaterally cause traffic shifts.

3) Rollback procedure (<10 minutes)
- On point: Victor + Emily.
- Steps (also added to the runbook draft):
  a) Revert the Terraform commit that changes TTL/record sets (PR-1442) and re-apply in prod workspace.
  b) Validate with `dig` from 3 external vantage points + internal DNS sampler (we already run this in CI; we’ll run manually).
  c) Confirm R53 change propagation via AWS console change ID.
- We tested the revert path in staging and timed it at ~6–8 minutes end-to-end (including validation).

4) Metrics / monitoring checklist
- Attached a one-pager checklist (see “monitoring-checklist-gameday-2026-01-15.md”). Highlights:
  - Gateway: 5xx rate, 429 rate, p95/p99 latency, retry-after count (Peter’s panel), request volume by region
  - DNS: Route 53 query count + health check status by region
  - Probes: perf-canary cross-region reachability + latency (Emily’s dashboard)
  - Failover controller: policy flip count (should be 0 today), dry-run recommendations (if anyone tests)
  - Annotations: we’ll add an explicit “Terraform apply” annotation to the failover dashboard once apply completes

If you want, we can narrow apply time to be earlier (closer to US morning) so more folks are around.

Victor

Attachment: monitoring-checklist-gameday-2026-01-15.md

text:
From: Victor Alvarez <victor.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@redwoodinference.com>, Miles Thornton <miles.thornton@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Sign-off request: DNS TTL + traffic steering updates for regional failover (Terraform PR-1442)

Hi Irene,

Requesting infra leadership sign-off for the DNS TTL + traffic steering changes we need before the controlled failover game day (US-East -> EU-West) tomorrow.

Change summary (PR-1442):
- Lower DNS TTL for api.redwoodinference.com and dedicated-gw.redwoodinference.com records from 60s -> 20s (Route 53 alias + health-checked records).
- Enable weighted routing policy targets for “failover-ready” regions (default 100/0, controller can flip to 0/100).
- Add explicit “hold-down” during flips (via routing controller; infra side is just supporting records + constraints).
- Add resolver-cache jitter note in runbook (we cannot control client resolvers, but lower TTL materially improves time-to-shift for the long tail).

Risk assessment (high level):
1) Risk: unintended traffic shift due to misconfigured record weights/health checks.
   Mitigation: staged apply in prod (plan-only review, then apply w/ Victor + Emily on bridge), hard-coded default weights pinned in Terraform, and a one-command rollback (revert commit + apply).

2) Risk: resolvers ignoring low TTL / caching longer, making failover appear “slow” and leading to repeated flips.
   Mitigation: controller anti-flap + rate limits (ADR-043); we will *not* treat “not all clients moved” as a failure condition within the first N minutes. Observability will track ‘traffic moved’ vs ‘resolvers still pinned’.

3) Risk: increased DNS query rate / cost.
   Mitigation: expected increase is bounded; we validated on staging + last 30d QPS. Also, TTL 20s is temporary until we have more confidence; can raise to 30–45s with no functional changes.

4) Risk: interacting with Anycast/BGP steering and provider edge behavior.
   Mitigation: we are not changing any BGP announcements. This is DNS-only + routing policy weights.

Proposed execution:
- Today: sign-off + Terraform apply in prod during low-traffic window (target 17:00–18:00 UTC).
- Verify: synthetic reachability probes + resolver sampling + gateway metrics.
- Tomorrow: game day will use controller dry-run first; only flip traffic with go/no-go from Sean.

Asks:
- Please reply with approval for prod apply of PR-1442 today.
- Any constraints you want us to add to the go/no-go or rollback criteria.

Attachments:
- dns-ttl-change-risk-assessment-v2.pdf
- terraform-plan-pr1442.txt

Thanks,
Victor Alvarez
Network Reachability Lead
Redwood Inference

title: Post-demo: benchmark links, prompt sets, and version safeguard

From: Ari Thompson <ari@lexicon.ai>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Desai <priya@lexicon.ai>
Date: Mon, 12 Jan 2026 14:03:00 -0500
Subject: Lexicon demo — model fit and quality checks

Hi Hannah,

Thanks for the demo today — the team found the latency conversation helpful and we liked the idea of the Dedicated offering for peak loads. A couple of follow-ups from our side as we evaluate fit:

- Could you share the benchmark comparison you mentioned (conversation-turn accuracy + hallucination checks) against the smaller open models?
- We have an existing prompt bank for our summarization and classification flows; would you take a quick look and let us know which model(s) from Redwood you think are a match?
- We're nervous about regressions after model updates — if we detect a drop in quality after a model version change, what are our rollback/pinning options? SLA for urgent fixes?

I'll attach our sample prompts and a handful of annotated examples for context (copied from the repo).

Next steps from us: confirm a 60-minute technical follow-up with your solutions engineer and legal will be looping in procurement for licensing questions.

Best,
Ari
Ari Thompson
Head of ML Product, Lexicon.ai
[Attachment: lexicon_prompt_samples.jsonl (JSONL, 48KB)]

From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah@redwood.com>
To: Ari Thompson <ari@lexicon.ai>
Cc: Priya Desai <priya@lexicon.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 12 Jan 2026 16:28:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Lexicon demo — model fit and quality checks

Hi Ari — great to meet you (virtually) today, thanks for sending the prompt bank. Attached is a short evaluation pack I put together from the sample prompts you sent. Quick notes up front:

- Recommended models: rdx-medium-1 (good latency/cost balance) and rdx-large-q4f for stricter hallucination control on summarization.
- Benchmarks: I exported a small comparison run that includes Rouge-L, factuality checks (lexical overlap + model-based fact-checker), and token cost breakdown — link to Drive below.
- Prompt sets: I mapped your sample prompts to recommended templates and included a small gold set for regression monitoring (prompt_set_v1.jsonl).
- Regression mitigation: Redwood supports model pinning per-route and automated canary rollouts. If you detect a regression after a model update we can: 1) pin the route to the previous exact model build (immutable id), 2) open a priority ticket to route requests to dedicated fallback capacity while we investigate, or 3) apply a transient prompt shim (we can surface suggested prompt-level offsets). Pinning is generally the fastest remediation.

Attachments: lexicon_eval_summary_20260112.csv, prompt_set_v1.jsonl, pin_request_template.pdf
Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aB_example_benchmarks
HubSpot: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/123456/record/LEX-2026-07

Next steps I suggest:
1) Book the 60-min technical deep dive with Sean (solutions eng) — can you do Wed 1/14 or Thu 1/15?
2) I'll draft a pin-request playbook and a short runbook for regression triage for your SREs.
3) If legal needs a sample SLA language for rollback/incident windows, Monica can surface precedent language we use for Dedicated customers.

Happy to hop on a quick call to walk through the CSV results.

Cheers,
Hannah Schmitt
Enterprise AE, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: lexicon_eval_summary_20260112.csv (CSV, 2.3MB)]

Thanks —
Hannah
