Redwood Inference



From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
To: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Jun 3, 2025 at 11:26 AM
Subject: Re: Fwd: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Cascade)

On rollback semantics (what we can say today):

- Supported upgrade path is N-1 → N for Redwood Private. We do not currently support skipping major versions without a review.
- Rollback is supported to the previously running version provided:
  (a) the upgrade hasn’t crossed a non-reversible schema boundary, and
  (b) the recommended backups/snapshots were taken before applying migrations.
- Our current guidance: take a pre-upgrade backup of the control-plane DB + config state; if on EBS/CSI, take a volume snapshot; if managed DB, take a point-in-time snapshot.
- For migration failures: we run migrations with guardrails (lock timeouts, idempotent steps), but in the event of a failure we can roll back application components immediately and then restore DB to the pre-upgrade snapshot.

What I’d like to avoid: “guaranteeing” a fixed rollback time without knowing their storage/backend. We can give a typical range and define prerequisites (snapshot ready, prechecks clean, artifacts available).

If helpful, we can offer a rollback drill in staging prior to prod.

Vivek

—
Vivek Kulkarni
Product, Private Deployments
Redwood Inference



From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>

Date: Tue, Jun 3, 2025 at 12:05 PM
Subject: Re: Fwd: Escalation: rollback guarantees + upgrade support coverage (Cascade)

Two notes for the written response:

1) Please avoid hard “guarantees” unless it’s in their contract. Use “supported rollback procedure” and “expected restoration time is typically X–Y given prerequisites.”

2) Since they’re regulated, mention auditability: we can provide an upgrade report (precheck results, timestamps for backup start/complete, and change summary). If they require SIEM evidence, we can provide audit log entries for upgrade start/stop + rollback invocation in our newer Private releases (verify what version they are on / targeting).

Naomi

Naomi Feldman
Security/Compliance Product Reviewer
Redwood Inference



From: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jun 4, 2025 at 9:18 AM
Subject: Draft response to Cascade (rollback + support coverage)

All — draft below. Please reply with edits/approvals. If no objections by 2pm PT I’ll send.

---
Alyssa,

Thanks — understood on the need for operational clarity.

Rollback (supported procedure)
- For Redwood Private, we support in-place upgrades on the supported path (N-1 → N). As part of the upgrade workflow, Redwood requires a pre-upgrade backup/snapshot of the control-plane database and configuration state.
- If an upgrade fails (including during database migrations), the supported remediation is:
  1) stop/roll back the application components to the prior running version, and
  2) restore the control-plane database from the pre-upgrade snapshot (if migrations were applied and need to be reverted).
- In environments with pre-upgrade snapshots in place, service restoration is typically on the order of tens of minutes. Exact timing depends on the snapshot mechanism (managed DB PITR vs volume snapshot) and the size of the database.

Upgrade window support coverage
- We can staff your planned maintenance window (Sat 1:00–4:00am ET) with Redwood support + SRE escalation.
- Proposed plan: you share precheck outputs and the target version 48 hours prior; we open a live bridge at the start of the window; Redwood SRE/release escalation is available throughout until completion or rollback confirmation.

Auditability
- We can provide an upgrade report containing precheck results and timestamps for backup/upgrade steps. If required, we can also provide audit log entries that reflect upgrade start/stop and rollback invocation (version dependent).

If you’d like, we can also run a rollback drill in a non-production environment prior to the production change window.

---

Markus

  1) roll back the application components to the prior running version, and
  2) restore the control-plane database from the pre-upgrade snapshot if migrations were applied and need to be reverted.
- With pre-upgrade snapshots in place, service restoration is typically on the order of tens of minutes. Exact timing depends on your snapshot mechanism (managed DB PITR vs volume snapshot) and the size of the database.

Date: Wed, Jun 4, 2025 at 9:18 AM
Subject: Draft response to Cascade (rollback + support coverage)

All — draft below. Please reply with edits/approvals. If no objections by 2pm PT I’ll send.

---
Alyssa,

Thanks — understood on the need for operational clarity.

Rollback (supported procedure)
- For Redwood Private, we support in-place upgrades on the supported path (N-1 → N). As part of the upgrade workflow, Redwood requires a pre-upgrade backup/snapshot of the control-plane database and configuration state.
- If an upgrade fails (including during database migrations), the supported remediation is:
  1) stop/roll back the application components to the prior running version, and
  2) restore the control-plane database from the pre-upgrade snapshot (if migrations were applied and need to be reverted).
- In environments with pre-upgrade snapshots in place, service restoration is typically on the order of tens of minutes. Exact timing depends on the snapshot mechanism (managed DB PITR vs volume snapshot) and the size of the database.

Upgrade window support coverage
- We can staff your planned maintenance window (Sat 1:00–4:00am ET) with Redwood support + SRE escalation.
- Proposed plan: you share precheck outputs and the target version 48 hours prior; we open a live bridge at the start of the window; Redwood SRE/release escalation is available throughout until completion or rollback confirmation.

Auditability
- We can provide an upgrade report containing precheck results and timestamps for backup/upgrade steps. If required, we can also provide audit log entries that reflect upgrade start/stop and rollback invocation (version dependent).

If you’d like, we can also run a rollback drill in a non-production environment prior to the production change window.

---

Markus

  1) roll back the application components to the prior running version, and
  2) restore the control-plane database from the pre-upgrade snapshot if migrations were applied and need to be reverted.
- With pre-upgrade snapshots in place, service restoration is typically on the order of tens of minutes. Exact timing depends on your snapshot mechanism (managed DB PITR vs volume snapshot) and the size of the database.

title: GPU pool provisioning kickoff and regional fallback decisions
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Marta Silva <marta@veridian.ai>, Ravi Kapoor <ravi.kapoor@veridian.ai>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 May 2025 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: GPU pool provisioning kickoff and initial asks

Hi Marta / Ravi —

Great to meet last week. Per our call, I’m opening a short thread to consolidate the kickoff actions for Veridian’s dedicated GPU pool. Our goal is to have a staged provisioning window (staging -> canary -> prod) and a clear regional fallback plan so your ops team can validate latency and failover before going live.

Quick context: Veridian expects steady inference at ~120 QPS (50-token average) for the first month with expected spikes up to ~380 QPS during peak workflows. Primary models will be Llama-2-family variants (13B / 70B) with a mix of streaming chat and batch rerank requests.

Immediate asks from our side so we can size and schedule provisioning:
- Confirm preferred primary region(s) (we suggest US-WEST for lowest latency + US-EAST as fallback). If data residency requires a single region, call that out.
- Confirm expected peak sustained tokens/min and peak burst windows (duration).
- Any compliance constraints (IP allowlists, traffic egress, KMS/HSM usage).
- Target SLA/latency SLO (p50/p95 desired).

Attachments: veridian_mcap_proposal.xlsx (initial sizing), mutual-action-plan.docx (MVP timeline + owners).

I’m looping Monica and Karthik to cover implementation and runtime design. Can we target a short discovery call tomorrow (30 minutes) to lock the region and burst expectations?

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0197

---


From: Marta Silva <marta@veridian.ai>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Ravi Kapoor <ravi.kapoor@veridian.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 15 May 2025 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: GPU pool provisioning kickoff and initial asks

Jordan —

Thanks for the clear summary and the attachments. A few quick answers before the call:
- Region: Veridian’s compliance team prefers US-WEST for production (our primary tenant data must remain in west coast AWS regions). We can allow US-EAST for staging/canary if traffic is encrypted and access is IAM-restricted.
- Traffic shape: baseline ~100–140 QPS; predictable daily peaks to ~350 QPS for ~20–45 minutes; unpredictable spikes up to ~500 QPS for short bursts (under 10 minutes) during promotional tests.
- Compliance: KMS is mandatory for any key material. We will need SOC2 evidence and access to audit logs for the dedicated pool.
- SLA: We target p95 inference latency under 450ms for 50-token responses. If that’s unrealistic at initial sizing, call that out and we’ll adjust expectations.

I’m available for a 30-min sync tomorrow at 11:00 PT. If that works, Monica — can you bring the staging checklist and the burst-policy outline?

Best,
Marta Silva
Head of Platform Operations | Veridian.ai
marta@veridian.ai

> Great to meet last week. Per our call, I’m opening a short thread to consolidate the kickoff actions for Veridian’s dedicated GPU pool...


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Marta Silva <marta@veridian.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 16 May 2025 14:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: GPU pool provisioning kickoff and initial asks — staging checklist & burst policy (draft)

Hi Marta / Jordan —

Thanks — confirming 11:00 PT tomorrow works for us. I’m attaching a draft staging-runbook.pdf that lays out the three-phase activation plan (staging -> canary -> prod), minimum acceptance criteria for each phase, and a short runbook for smoke tests. I’ve also attached a draft burst-policy-proposal.md showing two burst modes we typically recommend:

1) Elastic burst (preferred): autoscale up to 2.5x baseline for short spikes (<10 min) with prewarm of a small set of model slots. Quick cooldown to baseline; billing tracked separately as burst usage.
2) Reserved buffer + overflow: reserve capacity sized for 1.5x baseline and route overflow to a slightly lower-cost model variant in a different region (fallback).

Proposed acceptance criteria for moving from canary -> prod:
- p95 latency <= 450ms for 95% of canary traffic for 48h
- No more than 2 unhandled timeouts or model OOMs during the canary window
- KV cache hit rate >= 60% for chat workloads (we’ll instrument this)

Compliance/ops notes: I included a SOC2 evidence checklist (for your security team) and an IAM allowlist template. Karthik, can you flag any runtime limitations we should share with Veridian ahead of the call?

Attachments: staging-runbook.pdf, burst-policy-proposal.md

Thanks,
Monica Patel
Customer Success Manager | Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwood.com

On Thu, May 15, 2025 at 10:03 AM Marta Silva <marta@veridian.ai> wrote:
> Thanks for the clear summary and the attachments. A few quick answers before the call...


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Marta Silva <marta@veridian.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 18 May 2025 11:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: GPU pool provisioning kickoff and initial asks — technical notes

Marta / team —

Quick technical notes to set expectations ahead of provisioning:

1) Kernel selection & model variants: For the 70B models you mentioned, we recommend quantized variant (8-bit with selected FP16 kernels) for cost/latency balance. That will usually meet the p95 target but requires a short model warmup (natural in canary).

2) Prewarm & KV cache: To meet short spikes (10 minutes), we rely on prewarmed model slots + KV (prefix) caching. For chat workloads, we can prewarm ~20 pods (model slots) which covers most short bursts without a full autoscale cycle. Prewarm is charged as reserved capacity during the warm window.

3) Regional fallback design: Because Veridian requires US-WEST for prod, we’ll deploy primary capacity in US-WEST and stage a lean fallback in US-EAST that contains lower-cost variants and partial KV caches. Traffic will prefer US-WEST; on degraded health signals we failover to US-EAST with a transparent model variant mapping. Note: cross-region KV consistency will be eventual; recommend session affinity for critical flows.

4) Observability hooks: We’ll expose token-level cost breakdowns and a p95 latency dashboard. Monica — we’ll need a short list of synthetic prompts from Veridian to seed the canary tests (3–5 prompt sets per workload).

Open items for the call:
- Decision on prewarm size (20 pods suggested) and who will sign off on prewarm billing model.
- Whether Veridian wants cross-region KV replication (costly) or eventual consistency.

If helpful I can diagram the fallback path for the canary call.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Principal SW Engineer — Serving Runtime | Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com

title: Runtime LTS GA — go/no-go criteria and remaining risks
GA scope confirmation: Route-level Experiments (Console Rollouts) — April release train
preview:
From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Mar 20, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: GA scope confirmation: Route-level Experiments (Console Rollouts) — April release train
Attachments: Route_Experiments_GA_Scope_2025-03-20.pdf

Team — confirming GA scope + timeline for Route-level Experiments (“route A/B testing”) in Console Rollouts.

TL;DR
- Target: GA in April release train (Console 2025.04), with region-by-region enablement behind feature flag for the first 1–2 weeks.
- GA focus: safe operations + trustworthy metrics attribution + clear UX.
- Explicit non-goals for GA: full statistical significance engine, customer-defined custom metrics ingestion, cross-region population rebalancing.

GA scope (in)
1) Control plane + config model
- Experiment CRUD + state machine (draft → running → stopping/stopped), server-side validation.
- Deterministic assignment + eligibility rules per ADR-047.
- Stickiness key precedence finalized (SDK/header/customer_id fallbacks) + warnings when key entropy is low.

2) Data plane routing behavior
- Experiment selection applied at route policy evaluation, before fallback/canary resolution.
- Safe defaults if config unavailable (fail closed to control variant, do not “spray” traffic).

3) Telemetry + attribution
- Canonical attribution schema per ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region; model_id gated/sampled as discussed).
- Guardrails: attribution drop-rate monitoring + alerting.

4) Console
- Create/manage workflow (wizard), list/detail views.
- Results view with basic deltas (latency p50/p95/p99, error rate, throughput, cost/token) and guardrail status.
- Audit/change history view in Console.

5) Safety / compliance / ops
- RBAC enforced for mutations (create/update/start/stop/delete) + “break-glass” admin documented.
- Audit events for all lifecycle actions with required export fields for enterprise.
- Guardrails: soft-stop + notifications as default GA behavior.
- Optional hard-stop remains behind kill switch and disabled by default at GA (we can enable per-account later).

GA non-goals (explicit)
- No full statistical significance engine in-product (we can show deltas and guardrail thresholds only).
- No customer-managed custom metric ingestion (Redwood-defined metrics + Redwood eval signals only).
- No multi-region cross-population rebalancing beyond deterministic bucketing.

Open risks / required resourcing (asks)
- SRE: I need a committed block for alert review + game day + on-call readiness signoff (week of 3/31).
- Telemetry/data: we need attribution drop-rate consistently <0.5% in staging across streaming + retries; and a clear backfill plan for schema changes.
- Metrics cardinality: final SRE/Obs signoff on tagging restrictions before widening beta.

Requested confirmations by EOD tomorrow (Fri 3/21)
- Dylan: confirm eng cut line for April train + any scope cuts needed to hit quality bar.
- Sean: confirm SRE coverage for (1) alerts/runbooks, (2) game day, (3) GA go/no-go.
- Priya: confirm cardinality constraints + any “must haves” before we GA-enable by default.
- Logan: confirm data quality/backfill plan and target numbers for attribution drop + propagation lag.
- Marissa: confirm GA messaging (esp. “no stat-sig”) + beta→GA comms plan.

Thanks — attaching the one-pager we’ll use for exec staff sync so we’re aligned.

Evelyn Hart
VP Product, Redwood Inference


---

From: Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Mar 20, 2025 at 10:06 AM PT
Subject: Re: GA scope confirmation: Route-level Experiments (Console Rollouts) — April release train

Confirmed on scope as written.

Eng cut line proposal (Console 2025.04)
- Keep: results API basic deltas + guardrail banners; audit trail view; RBAC gates on all mutations.
- Keep: assignment determinism + “missing/low-entropy sticky key” warnings (we saw too much skew without this).
- Keep: query cost controls (aggregation-backed charts only; no raw high-cardinality drilldowns at GA).

Scope we should explicitly de-prioritize to protect schedule
- Anything resembling in-Console statistical significance. We should close the loop on the exploratory branch and ensure no UI hooks imply it.
- “Quality” UX beyond showing the eval score stream if present (no deep analysis). If we’re tight, we can display quality as a single normalized score trend per variant and ship richer breakdown later.

Risk callouts
- Results query load: we need the aggregation jobs stable + cached endpoints. If we miss that, Console page performance will regress under larger beta.
- Attribution completeness for streaming + retries: we’re dependent on the last fixes + validation in staging.

If you want a hard GA gate, I’d suggest: (1) attribution drop <0.5%, (2) propagation lag p99 within SLO, (3) no P1 alerts for 7 days during beta expansion.

-Dylan
Engineering Lead, Console & Tooling

text:
From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Dylan Brooks <dylan_brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan_wright@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Mar 20, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: GA scope confirmation: Route-level Experiments (Console Rollouts) — April release train
Attachments: Route_Experiments_GA_Scope_2025-03-20.pdf

Team — confirming GA scope + timeline for Route-level Experiments (“route A/B testing”) in Console Rollouts.

TL;DR
- Target: GA in April release train (Console 2025.04), with region-by-region enablement behind feature flag for the first 1–2 weeks.
- GA focus: safe operations + trustworthy metrics attribution + clear UX.
- Explicit non-goals for GA: full statistical significance engine, customer-defined custom metrics ingestion, cross-region population rebalancing.

GA scope (in)
1) Control plane + config model
- Experiment CRUD + state machine (draft → running → stopping/stopped), server-side validation.
- Deterministic assignment + eligibility rules per ADR-047.
- Stickiness key precedence finalized (SDK/header/customer_id fallbacks) + warnings when key entropy is low.

2) Data plane routing behavior
- Experiment selection applied at route policy evaluation, before fallback/canary resolution.
- Safe defaults if config unavailable (fail closed to control variant, do not “spray” traffic).

3) Telemetry + attribution
- Canonical attribution schema per ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region; model_id gated/sampled as discussed).
- Guardrails: attribution drop-rate monitoring + alerting.

4) Console
- Create/manage workflow (wizard), list/detail views.
- Results view with basic deltas (latency p50/p95/p99, error rate, throughput, cost/token) and guardrail status.
- Audit/change history view in Console.

5) Safety / compliance / ops
- RBAC enforced for mutations (create/update/start/stop/delete) + “break-glass” admin documented.
- Audit events for all lifecycle actions with required export fields for enterprise.
- Guardrails: soft-stop + notifications as default GA behavior.
- Optional hard-stop remains behind kill switch and disabled by default at GA (we can enable per-account later).

GA non-goals (explicit)
- No full statistical significance engine in-product (we can show deltas and guardrail thresholds only).
- No customer-managed custom metric ingestion (Redwood-defined metrics + Redwood eval signals only).
- No multi-region cross-population rebalancing beyond deterministic bucketing.

Open risks / required resourcing (asks)
- SRE: I need a committed block for alert review + game day + on-call readiness signoff (week of 3/31).
- Telemetry/data: we need attribution drop-rate consistently <0.5% in staging across streaming + retries; and a clear backfill plan for schema changes.
- Metrics cardinality: final SRE/Obs signoff on tagging restrictions before widening beta.

Requested confirmations by EOD tomorrow (Fri 3/21)
- Dylan: confirm eng cut line for April train + any scope cuts needed to hit quality bar.
- Sean: confirm SRE coverage for (1) alerts/runbooks, (2) game day, (3) GA go/no-go.
- Priya: confirm cardinality constraints + any “must haves” before we GA-enable by default.
- Logan: confirm data quality/backfill plan and target numbers for attribution drop + propagation lag.
- Marissa: confirm GA messaging (esp. “no stat-sig”) + beta→GA comms plan.

Thanks — attaching the one-pager we’ll use for exec staff sync so we’re aligned.

Evelyn Hart
VP Product, Redwood Inference

title: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)
Attachments: CacheTelemetrySprint_OnePager_2025-03-03.pdf; CacheMetricsCardinalityBudget_v6.xlsx

Team — requesting go/no-go today so we can start implementation tomorrow.

Proposed sprint: Prefix + KV cache telemetry + initial “increase hit-rate” recommendations MVP (internal report / minimal Console stub).

Scope (2–3 weeks):
1) Runtime instrumentation (prefix cache + KV cache):
   - hit/miss counters + partial-hit semantics (per ADR)
   - miss reason taxonomy (stable set)
   - eviction + memory pressure signals
   - wasted prefill metric (privacy-safe, bucketed)
   - sampling + local rate limiting
2) Joinability/tags from gateway/control plane:
   - model_id + model_version_id, route_id, deployment_mode, region
   - strict allowlists; no per-tenant high-cardinality labels in raw metrics
3) Ingestion + cardinality controls:
   - enforce label policy + drop/aggregate forbidden label combos
   - sampling strategy to keep cost stable
4) Dashboards/alerts + runbook:
   - SRE dashboards for triage and regression detection
   - guardrail SLO proposal (internal; no customer SLA)
5) Recommendations MVP:
   - daily batch job that produces per-route/actionable levers (cache bytes/TTL/policy/routing locality/prompt canonicalization)

Key risks (need explicit sign-off):
- Metric cardinality/cost blowup if labels drift (route/model/version alone can be high)
- Runtime overhead: must be near-zero on p95/p99
- Privacy: no raw prompts; only bucketed lengths + hashed/canonicalized features
- Cross-system join correctness (model version IDs, route IDs)

Acceptance gates (proposal):
- Perf canary: p95 latency regression <0.5% at default sampling; no measurable throughput drop
- Cardinality: within budget in attached sheet; ingestion drops/guards tested in staging
- Data QA: dashboards show sane counts across Hosted + Dedicated canary; missing-tag rate <1% on canary

Owners (draft):
- Runtime instrumentation: Omar (lead), Samir (impl), Noah (review), Sofia (approval)
- Platform tags/join keys: Ethan + Amara
- Ingestion/cardinality: Logan (lead) + Nadia + Caleb
- Dashboards/alerts/runbook: Allison (lead) + Sergio + Aisha; approval: Sean
- Perf/overhead: Marcus (owner)
- Rollout/canary: Connor
- Program mgmt: Talia

If approved, I’d like a single-thread “go” from each of you (Runtime, Platform/Telemetry, Reliability/SRE) + any hard constraints we need to bake into the plan.

Thanks,
Ava
CTO, Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 9:34 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Runtime.

Hard constraints from our side:
- Instrumentation MUST be behind config flags, default on only where we’ve validated overhead (staging/canary first).
- Streaming retries + continuous batching semantics have to be correct (no double counting). We’ve been burned here.
- Miss reason taxonomy needs to be stable; don’t iterate labels every other day or SRE dashboards will churn.

Omar is set as lead; Noah will review the final metric definitions vs the ADR before we flip anything beyond canary.

Mateo
VP Engineering, Runtime


---

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 10:02 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Platform/Telemetry with two conditions:

1) Cardinality policy is non-negotiable:
   - allowlist labels only; no “free-form route name”, no tenant_id, no request_id
   - route_id/model_version_id must be normalized (single source of truth from control plane)
   - prompt-length/token-count must be bucketed (per standards)

2) Ingestion needs an explicit “drop detection” signal.
   We need to know when guards are dropping series so dashboards don’t show false “0 hits”. Please make that part of ENG-4823/4825 acceptance.

Logan/Nadia will own schema enforcement; Caleb will implement the guardrails.

Priya
VP Engineering, Platform


---

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 10:29 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Reliability.

A few guardrails:
- Please treat this as an operational change. Rollout needs canary gates + rollback plan (Connor + Sean to sign off before prod enablement).
- I want “telemetry pipeline health” on the same dashboard page as cache hit-rate. When data is missing, we need a clear indicator.
- Any p99 regression >1% at canary should block rollout unless explicitly approved by Runtime + SRE.

If we hit these gates, I’m comfortable proceeding.

Neha Kapoor
VP Infrastructure & Reliability


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 11:11 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

+1 go from SRE.

Specific SRE asks (so we don’t page ourselves to death):
- Alerts must be “guardrail” and avoid paging on workload mix shifts. Start with ticket-level alerts and promote to page only after we see stable baselines.
- Add an alert on “hit-rate appears to be zero because metrics are missing” (drop detection + missing-tag).
- Runbook must include:
  - how to validate join keys (route_id/model_version_id)
  - how to distinguish real regression vs telemetry failure
  - rollback steps and who owns each step

Allison will drive dashboards + runbook; I’ll approve alert policy before prod.

Sean
Director, Site Reliability Engineering


---

From: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 12:03 PM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Thanks all.

From Product side: +1 to proceed. The minimal requirements for the “recommendations MVP” slice are:
- Output is per route + model_version + region (at minimum) and includes a plain-English “why” with suggested levers.
- Must include caveats when confidence is low (e.g., insufficient sample, high workload volatility).
- We should explicitly avoid any recommendation that implies logging prompt content.

If we can land a first internal report + 1–2 example recommendations validated by CS, that’s a win for this sprint.

Paula
Sr PM, Telemetry Requirements / Rollouts


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 2:06 PM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Approved — thank you.

Decisions captured:
- Proceed with the sprint starting tomorrow.
- Must ship behind flags; staging -> canary -> prod only with gates.
- Cardinality policy enforced via allowlists + ingestion guards; add explicit drop-detection metrics.
- Perf gates: p95 <0.5% regression at default sampling; p99 >1% at canary blocks unless Runtime+SRE approve.
- Alerts start ticket-level; page only after baseline stabilization.
- Runbook + telemetry pipeline health indicator are required deliverables.

Talia: please set up a daily 15-min leadership checkpoint this week only (Mon–Fri) to keep decision latency low.

Connor/Allison/Logan/Omar: please reply-all by EOD with the concrete rollout timeline (staging date, canary start, target prod date) + explicit rollback owner.

text:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 9:12 AM PT
Subject: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)
Attachments: CacheTelemetrySprint_OnePager_2025-03-03.pdf; CacheMetricsCardinalityBudget_v6.xlsx

Team — requesting go/no-go today so we can start implementation tomorrow.

Proposed sprint: Prefix + KV cache telemetry + initial “increase hit-rate” recommendations MVP (internal report / minimal Console stub).

Scope (2–3 weeks):
1) Runtime instrumentation (prefix cache + KV cache):
   - hit/miss counters + partial-hit semantics (per ADR)
   - miss reason taxonomy (stable set)
   - eviction + memory pressure signals
   - wasted prefill metric (privacy-safe, bucketed)
   - sampling + local rate limiting
2) Joinability/tags from gateway/control plane:
   - model_id + model_version_id, route_id, deployment_mode, region
   - strict allowlists; no per-tenant high-cardinality labels in raw metrics
3) Ingestion + cardinality controls:
   - enforce label policy + drop/aggregate forbidden label combos
   - sampling strategy to keep cost stable
4) Dashboards/alerts + runbook:
   - SRE dashboards for triage and regression detection
   - guardrail SLO proposal (internal; no customer SLA)
5) Recommendations MVP:
   - daily batch job that produces per-route/actionable levers (cache bytes/TTL/policy/routing locality/prompt canonicalization)

Key risks (need explicit sign-off):
- Metric cardinality/cost blowup if labels drift (route/model/version alone can be high)
- Runtime overhead: must be near-zero on p95/p99
- Privacy: no raw prompts; only bucketed lengths + hashed/canonicalized features
- Cross-system join correctness (model version IDs, route IDs)

Acceptance gates (proposal):
- Perf canary: p95 latency regression <0.5% at default sampling; no measurable throughput drop
- Cardinality: within budget in attached sheet; ingestion drops/guards tested in staging
- Data QA: dashboards show sane counts across Hosted + Dedicated canary; missing-tag rate <1% on canary

Owners (draft):
- Runtime instrumentation: Omar (lead), Samir (impl), Noah (review), Sofia (approval)
- Platform tags/join keys: Ethan + Amara
- Ingestion/cardinality: Logan (lead) + Nadia + Caleb
- Dashboards/alerts/runbook: Allison (lead) + Sergio + Aisha; approval: Sean
- Perf/overhead: Marcus (owner)
- Rollout/canary: Connor
- Program mgmt: Talia

If approved, I’d like a single-thread “go” from each of you (Runtime, Platform/Telemetry, Reliability/SRE) + any hard constraints we need to bake into the plan.

Thanks,
Ava
CTO, Redwood Inference


---

From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 9:34 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Runtime.

Hard constraints from our side:
- Instrumentation MUST be behind config flags, default on only where we’ve validated overhead (staging/canary first).
- Streaming retries + continuous batching semantics have to be correct (no double counting). We’ve been burned here.
- Miss reason taxonomy needs to be stable; don’t iterate labels every other day or SRE dashboards will churn.

Omar is set as lead; Noah will review the final metric definitions vs the ADR before we flip anything beyond canary.

Mateo
VP Engineering, Runtime

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Mateo Alvarez <mateo.alvarez@redwoodinference.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Paula Mendes <paula.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Omar El-Sayed <omar.elsayed@redwoodinference.com>, Logan Wright <logan.wright@redwoodinference.com>, Allison Grant <allison.grant@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Talia Benson <talia.benson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Mar 3, 2025 at 10:02 AM PT
Subject: Re: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint — go/no-go (prefix/KV cache telemetry + recommendations MVP)

Go from Platform/Telemetry with two conditions:

1) Cardinality policy is non-negotiable:
   - allowlist labels only; no “free-form route name”, no tenant_id, no request_id
   - route_id/model_version_id must be normalized (single source of truth from control plane)
   - prompt-length/token-count must be bucketed (per standards)

2) Ingestion needs an explicit “drop detection” signal.

title: Unit Economics dashboard — readiness for quarterly KPI reporting + data quality risks
Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)
preview:
From: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 9:18 AM PT
Subject: Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)

Team — we need to lock the behavior for guardrails in beta/GA so Console copy + backend semantics match.

Question: when a guardrail triggers (e.g., p99 latency regression, error rate spike, cost/token blowup), do we automatically stop the experiment (“hard-stop”), or do we mark as degraded + notify and require operator action (“soft-stop”)?

Proposal (recommended):
1) Initial release (beta + GA): SOFT-STOP only
- System evaluates guardrails continuously and sets experiment.status = DEGRADED when triggered
- Console banner + notifications (email + Slack integration later) with “Stop experiment” CTA
- No routing behavior change automatically
- Audit event emitted with metric + threshold that tripped

2) Follow-on (post-GA, optional): HARD-STOP as an opt-in per experiment
- Behind an account-level feature flag + global kill switch
- Default off; requires explicit opt-in in the creation wizard
- If triggered, transitions experiment to STOPPING -> STOPPED, and routing reverts to control / pre-experiment policy

Rationale for soft-stop first:
- We don’t yet have enough operational confidence in thresholds and attribution stability to terminate traffic automatically
- False positives are costly (we’d be flipping customer production behavior based on noisy metrics)
- Stop semantics are not instantaneous (propagation lag, cache staleness) so we can’t promise “immediate stop” anyway
- We can still be “safe” via very loud degraded state + recommended actions + runbook links

Open items if we go soft-stop first:
- What language do we put in GA docs (“guardrails detect regressions” vs “guardrails prevent regressions”)
- What is the minimal “severe regression” policy we want to support later (e.g., p99 > X% for Y min)

Attachments:
- Route-Experiments-Guardrails-Decision-Memo-2025-02-12.pdf
- Guardrails-Thresholds-Proposal-v3.csv

If everyone’s aligned, I’ll update ENG-9028 scope and we’ll keep the hard-stop work as follow-on behind a flag.

— Dylan
Dylan Brooks
Engineering Lead, Console & Tooling
Redwood Inference


From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 10:02 AM PT
Subject: Re: Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)

+1 to soft-stop for beta/GA.

Two SRE-specific reasons to avoid hard-stop by default:
- We’ve already seen attribution drop in a few retry/streaming paths in staging. If attribution drops, guardrails can “flip” from OK -> BAD inaccurately (or vice versa). We shouldn’t take automated action until we’re confident the signal is stable.
- Hard-stop creates a new class of incident: customers asking why an experiment stopped “by itself” (and whether control plane is making business decisions). That will be hard to support while we’re still tuning alerts + dashboards.

If/when we do hard-stop:
- Must be gated by a global kill switch (SRE-owned)
- Must write the runbook before enabling
- Must expose “reason” in Console (which metric, window, threshold, and the underlying data freshness)

Sean
Sean Gallagher
Head of SRE
Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 10:37 AM PT
Subject: Re: Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)

Aligned.

From a product/expectations standpoint, we should be explicit:
- “Guardrails detect regressions and alert operators” (GA)
- We should avoid promising “automatic rollback” in GA launch messaging.

Console UX:
- We’ll show a degraded status with recommended actions and link out to the runbook
- We’ll keep “Auto-stop” copy out of the wizard for GA unless the backend semantics are actually there

I’ll update the PRD language today once decision is final.

Marissa
Marissa Cole
Product Lead, Console & Rollouts
Redwood Inference


From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 11:11 AM PT
Subject: Re: Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)

+1 soft-stop first.

One more angle: metric freshness / propagation lag.
- If aggregation jobs are delayed (or configs propagate slowly), the guardrail evaluator can compare mismatched windows.
- In that world, auto-stop can create “yo-yo” behavior: stop, restart, stop again.

If we add hard-stop later, please require:
- minimum evaluation window + cool-down (no repeated stop/start churn)
- explicit kill switch
- minimum data quality checks (attribution drop rate under threshold)

Priya
Priya Natarajan
Observability & Reliability
Redwood Inference


From: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 1:26 PM PT
Subject: Re: Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)

Evelyn — quick exec sign-off ask so we can lock GA semantics.

Decision proposal:
- Beta + GA: guardrails are SOFT-STOP (degraded state + notifications + operator stop action). No automatic stopping/rollback.
- Follow-on: optional HARD-STOP (opt-in per experiment) behind account flag + global kill switch; requires SRE runbook + data quality gating.

We’ll ensure the UI/Docs say “detect + alert” (not “automatic rollback”) for GA.

Can you reply with approval / any changes?

— Dylan


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Feb 12, 2025 at 2:07 PM PT
Subject: Re: Route experiments guardrails: soft-stop vs hard-stop (decision)

Approved as written.

Let’s ship soft-stop in GA, with clear degraded status + audit trail and strong operator guidance.

For hard-stop: I’m supportive, but only as an explicitly opt-in capability with an SRE-owned kill switch and after we’ve demonstrated stable attribution + low-noise thresholds with beta customers.

Evelyn
Evelyn Hart
VP Product
Redwood Inference

title: Dedicated capacity options and ramp pathway — Neurolabs
preview:
From: Daniel Park <daniel@neurolabs.ai>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: 2024-11-12T09:15:00-08:00
Subject: Dedicated capacity inquiry — throughput & ramp

Hi Sean,

We're getting close to ramping our user-facing assistant into production (Q1 2025) and need help sizing a Dedicated reservation that keeps latency predictable while optimizing cost. High-level:
- Peak concurrent sessions: ~1,200 (with 1.2k tokens/session average during bursts)
- Steady-state qps: ~120 requests/minute; bursts up to ~1,200 requests/minute for short windows
- SLA target: p95 < 350ms on generation-only traffic; some calls will be multi-turn chat (KV cache matters)
- Geo: primarily US-west + some east coast redundancy later

Can you put together 2–3 concrete reservation options (cluster size, minimum reserved GPUs, burst capacity, recommended ramp schedule over 3–9 months) and a rough monthly unit-cost estimate and assumptions? Also include any prewarm/warmup guidance we should expect during onboarding. I've attached a conservative forecast (neurolabs-forecast-2025.xlsx).

Thanks — happy to jump on a call next week.

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel@neurolabs.ai>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2024-11-13T08:30:00-08:00
Subject: Redwood Dedicated — proposed reservation variants (initial sizing)

Daniel / Sean — following our quick sync notes, here are three distinct reservation variants tailored to Neurolabs' forecast and latency goals. These are pragmatic starting points with operational notes. Assumptions: model = open LLM v2-large-equivalent; avg tokens/generated call = 1.2k; we assume continuous KV cache for multi-turn chat. Pricing is rough and will be refined once we lock model quantization and sustained sequence lengths.

Option 1 — Pilot Slate (low risk, fast validation)
- Cluster: 8x A100-class GPUs reserved (min-active)
- Burst capacity: up to 16 GPUs via auto-burst (subject to availability)
- Expected steady throughput: ~150 r/s for short generations (sufficient for baseline traffic)
- Ramp: 0–4 weeks internal validation, 4–12 weeks progressive traffic shift to 30% production
- Cost: estimated $65k/month (reservation + burst amortized); includes 1 week warmup credits
- Notes: Best if you want to validate prefix caching and hot-KV hit rates before large commit.

Option 2 — Anchored Production Slate (recommended for p95 targets)
- Cluster: 24x A100-class GPUs reserved (min-active)
- Burst capacity: 40 GPUs for peak windows via shared burst pool
- Expected steady throughput: supports target p95 < 350ms at projected concurrency with KV caching and batching tuned
- Ramp: staged activation over 8–12 weeks (8 weeks on core traffic, week 9–12 full roll to 100%)
- Cost: estimated $180k/month
- Notes: Includes prewarmed instances, conservative queue sizing, and 2 months of warmup credits; recommend a 90-day performance validation window tied to SLA clause.

Option 3 — Runway + Buffer (enterprisey, safety-first)
- Cluster: 40x A100-class GPUs reserved (min-active); dedicated redundancy capacity across two US regions (active-passive)
- Burst capacity: 60 GPUs (immediate spike absorption)
- Ramp: 12–24 weeks phased (pilot -> 50% -> 100%); optional warmstandby in region-2 after week 12
- Cost: estimated $285k/month
- Notes: For customers prioritizing uninterrupted availability during unpredictable spikes. Comes with SLA addendum and runbook for cross-region failover.

Operational recommendations across all options:
- Min GPUs should be sized so that sustained KV-hit workloads avoid frequent KV cache eviction; we recommend keeping ~20–25% headroom above steady-state token throughput for p95 safety.
- Use aggressive prefix/KV caching and 8–16 token batching windows to reduce per-token GPU load while keeping p95 targets.
- Warmup plan: progressive traffic steering + staged canary; we can provide a warmup playbook.
- Canary & fallback: plan for automatic fallback to smaller model variants if queue depth exceeds threshold.

Attached: redwood-dedicated-options.pdf (summary deck with sizing graphs and assumptions).

If you want, we can baseline a short performance test (2–3 days) on Option 1 to validate latencies and scaling multipliers before committing to Option 2.

—Kimberly
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood-dedicated-options.pdf (PDF deck)

Cc: Amira Khan <amira@neurolabs.ai>

3) SLA credits: what's the practical process and cadence for credits if we breach latency SLO tied to provisioning issues?

Also attaching a slightly updated forecast (I highlighted a couple of higher burst days). If we go ahead, we'd want a concrete mutual action plan (dates, who owns what) and a migration runbook.

—Daniel

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel@neurolabs.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
Date: 2024-11-20T11:02:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Redwood Dedicated — proposed reservation variants (initial sizing)

Daniel — good questions. Actionable summary & next steps below so we have a clean MAP and timelines:

MAP (mutual action plan)
- Week 0 (Nov 21–27): Agreement on target option (Pilot / Anchored / Runway). Redwood to provide final numbered quote and SLA addendum. (Owner: Sean + Marissa)
- Week 1–2 (Nov 28–Dec 11): Performance test window on Pilot Slate (if chosen) or limited canary on Anchored Slate. Redwood provides warmup playbook and runbook. (Owner: Kimberly)
- Week 3–8 (Dec 12–Jan 31): Phased activation and traffic shift; weekly check-ins and token-level latency reports. (Owner: Neurolabs product + Redwood SE)
- Week 9–12 (Feb 1–Feb 28): Full production roll, SLA go-live trigger. (Owner: Neurolabs + Redwood ops)

Answers to your follow-ups:
- Option 2 guaranteed floor during 6–8pm PT: we recommend reserving 24 GPUs as the hard minimum; during peak we'll reserve an additional 12 GPUs as a committed burst allocation for those windows (effectively ensuring 36 GPUs available for peak windows you flag in advance).
- Option 3 second-region cost: warm-standby in us-east (passive) adds ~20% incremental monthly spend vs single-region Option 3; active-active ~45% incremental — Kimberly will send a line-item breakout.
- SLA credits process: we track token-level p95 per contract period. If a provisioning incident causes SLO breach and is verified in the triage report, credits are applied per the SLA schedule; typical turnaround for initial investigation is 48–72 hours and credit application within 2 billing cycles. We'll include sample language in the quote.

Next immediate asks from us: please confirm which option you want to price formally and whether warm-standby is desired. We'll attach the quote and DocuSign for signature once you confirm.

Links: HubSpot deal — https://hubspot.example.com/deals/4592; MAP/runbook template — https://app.redwood.example.com/docs/ramp-runbook-template

Thanks — looking forward to closing this out.

Best,
Sean Gallagher
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
